subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — Wickard v. Filburn (1942)
stimulus:
The Agricultural Adjustment Act of 1938 was a law passed by Congress during the Great Depression. The purpose of the federal law was to regulate the production of wheat to stabilize the economy and the nation's food supply. The law limited the amount of wheat that farmers could grow on their farms. Roscoe Filburn, a farmer in Ohio, grew more wheat than the amount allowed under the Agricultural Adjustment Act. When Filburn was penalized under the law, he sued.

Filburn argued that the excess wheat grown on his farm was for personal use and therefore did not fall under the wheat limit established by the law. Filburn also argued that the law was unconstitutional because Congress did not have the authority to regulate how much wheat a farmer could grow for personal consumption or use.

A unanimous Supreme Court held that the power of Congress granted by Article I of the Constitution includes the authority to regulate activities in a single state when they have even an indirect effect on the economy of other states. That meant that the Agricultural Adjustment Act applied to Filburn's wheat sold at market and his wheat grown for personal use.

parts.prompt: Identify the constitutional clause that is common to both United States v. Lopez (1995) and Wickard v. Filburn (1942).
parts.scoring_notes: Only the Commerce Clause earns the point.

scoring_points.description: Identify the constitutional clause that is common to both United States v. Lopez (1995) and Wickard v. Filburn (1942).

required_elements: 1 pt: Identifies the Commerce Clause as the constitutional clause common to both cases.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Identifies the Commerce Clause as the constitutional clause common to both cases.

parts.prompt: Explain how the facts in United States v. Lopez and Wickard v. Filburn led to different holdings.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires accurate description of Lopez. Second point requires connecting both cases — in Lopez the activity was non-economic (gun possession) so Congress lacked Commerce Clause authority; in Wickard the activity (wheat growing) had an indirect economic effect on interstate commerce so Congress could regulate it.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in United States v. Lopez and Wickard v. Filburn led to different holdings. (1 point — describe relevant fact/holding about the required Supreme Court case)

required_elements: 1 pt (first point): Describes relevant information about the required Supreme Court case United States v. Lopez. Accept: in Lopez, a student was found to have possessed a weapon on school grounds; in United States v. Lopez, the Court held that gun possession was not an economic activity that could be considered interstate commerce.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt (first point): Describes relevant information about the required Supreme Court case United States v. Lopez. Accept: in Lopez, a student was found to have possessed a weapon on school grounds; in United States v. Lopez, the Court held that gun possession was not an economic activity that could be considered interstate commerce.

common_traps: Describing only one case without explaining the comparison does not earn the 2-point tier

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in United States v. Lopez and Wickard v. Filburn led to different holdings. (2 points — correctly explains how facts of BOTH cases led to different/similar holdings)

required_elements: 2 pts (second point): Correctly explains how the facts of both cases led to different holdings. Accept: in Lopez, a gun was brought into a school and the Court held this was not an economic activity and therefore not subject to the Commerce Clause; however, in Wickard v. Filburn, the Court determined Filburn could be punished under federal law for growing excess wheat for personal consumption because it could have an indirect effect on the economy of other states and was therefore covered by the Commerce Clause.
alternatives.correct_example: 2 pts (second point): Correctly explains how the facts of both cases led to different holdings. Accept: in Lopez, a gun was brought into a school and the Court held this was not an economic activity and therefore not subject to the Commerce Clause; however, in Wickard v. Filburn, the Court determined Filburn could be punished under federal law for growing excess wheat for personal consumption because it could have an indirect effect on the economy of other states and was therefore covered by the Commerce Clause.

common_traps: Describing only one case earns 1 point, not 2
Must connect facts to different outcomes

parts.prompt: Explain how the holding in Wickard v. Filburn reflects the concept of federalism.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain how the Wickard ruling relates to the federal-state power relationship — specifically that it expanded federal power to regulate even intrastate activities with indirect interstate effects.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the holding in Wickard v. Filburn reflects the concept of federalism.

required_elements: 1 pt: Explains how Wickard reflects federalism. Accept: in Wickard, the Supreme Court held that economic activity in one state could be regulated by the federal government when it impacts the economic activity of another state; the holding in Wickard shows that the struggle for power between the states and the federal government sometimes favors the federal government — the Court made activities performed entirely in one state subject to the Commerce Clause.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Explains how Wickard reflects federalism. Accept: in Wickard, the Supreme Court held that economic activity in one state could be regulated by the federal government when it impacts the economic activity of another state; the holding in Wickard shows that the struggle for power between the states and the federal government sometimes favors the federal government — the Court made activities performed entirely in one state subject to the Commerce Clause.
